rules.title: Federal Question Jurisdiction
rules.prompt_question: When does federal question jurisdiction exist?
rules.rule_statement: Federal question jurisdiction exists when the plaintiff's well pleaded complaint shows that the claim arises under federal law.
rules.application_example: If the complaint itself alleges a claim created by a federal statute, federal question jurisdiction exists.
rules.common_trap: A federal defense does not create federal question jurisdiction.

rules.title: Diversity Jurisdiction
rules.prompt_question: What are the requirements for diversity jurisdiction?
rules.rule_statement: Diversity jurisdiction exists when there is complete diversity between opposing parties and the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs.
rules.application_example: If every plaintiff is a citizen of a different state from every defendant and the claim exceeds $75,000, diversity jurisdiction exists.
rules.common_trap: Minimal diversity is not enough for ordinary diversity jurisdiction.

rules.title: Complete Diversity
rules.prompt_question: What does complete diversity require?
rules.rule_statement: Complete diversity requires that no plaintiff share state citizenship with any defendant.
rules.application_example: If one plaintiff and one defendant are both citizens of Texas, complete diversity is destroyed.
rules.common_trap: You compare every plaintiff against every defendant.

rules.title: Citizenship of Individuals
rules.prompt_question: How is an individual's citizenship determined for diversity jurisdiction?
rules.rule_statement: An individual is a citizen of the state in which the individual is domiciled.
rules.application_example: A person living in Illinois with intent to remain there indefinitely is a citizen of Illinois.
rules.common_trap: Residence alone is not enough. Citizenship depends on domicile.

rules.title: Change of Domicile
rules.prompt_question: When does a person change domicile?
rules.rule_statement: A person changes domicile only by being physically present in the new state and intending to remain there indefinitely.
rules.application_example: A student temporarily living in New York for school may remain domiciled in another state if there is no intent to remain indefinitely.
rules.common_trap: Temporary presence does not by itself change domicile.

rules.title: Citizenship of Corporations
rules.prompt_question: How is a corporation's citizenship determined for diversity jurisdiction?
rules.rule_statement: A corporation is a citizen of every state in which it is incorporated and the one state where it has its principal place of business.
rules.application_example: A corporation incorporated in Delaware with its principal place of business in California is a citizen of Delaware and California.
rules.common_trap: A corporation can have multiple incorporation citizenships but only one principal place of business.

rules.title: Principal Place of Business
rules.prompt_question: How is a corporation's principal place of business usually determined?
rules.rule_statement: A corporation's principal place of business is usually its nerve center, the place where high level officers direct, control, and coordinate corporate activities.
rules.application_example: If executives direct corporate operations from New Jersey, that state is usually the nerve center.
rules.common_trap: Large business activity in a state does not necessarily make that state the principal place of business.

rules.title: Citizenship of Unincorporated Associations
rules.prompt_question: How is the citizenship of an unincorporated association determined?
rules.rule_statement: An unincorporated association takes the citizenship of all of its members.
rules.application_example: An LLC is generally treated as a citizen of every state of which any member is a citizen.
rules.common_trap: Do not use the corporation rule for LLCs and partnerships.

rules.title: Amount in Controversy
rules.prompt_question: What amount in controversy is required for diversity jurisdiction?
rules.rule_statement: The amount in controversy must exceed $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs.
rules.application_example: A claim for exactly $75,000 does not satisfy the requirement.
rules.common_trap: The amount must exceed $75,000, not equal it.

rules.title: Legal Certainty Test
rules.prompt_question: When is the amount in controversy requirement not satisfied even if the complaint demands more than $75,000?
rules.rule_statement: The amount in controversy requirement is not satisfied if it appears to a legal certainty that the plaintiff cannot recover more than $75,000.
rules.application_example: If a statute caps damages below $75,000, the amount requirement is not met.
rules.common_trap: The court usually accepts the plaintiff's good faith allegation unless legal certainty defeats it.

rules.title: Aggregation by One Plaintiff
rules.prompt_question: When may a single plaintiff aggregate claims to satisfy the amount in controversy requirement?
rules.rule_statement: A single plaintiff may aggregate all claims against a single defendant to satisfy the amount in controversy requirement, whether or not the claims are related.
rules.application_example: A plaintiff may combine a contract claim and a tort claim against the same defendant.
rules.common_trap: This rule is broader than many students think. The claims need not be related.

rules.title: Aggregation by Multiple Plaintiffs
rules.prompt_question: May multiple plaintiffs aggregate separate claims to meet the amount in controversy requirement?
rules.rule_statement: Multiple plaintiffs generally may not aggregate separate and distinct claims to satisfy the amount in controversy requirement, but they may aggregate if they share a common undivided interest.
rules.application_example: Co-owners claiming a single undivided property interest may aggregate, but separate personal injury claims may not.
rules.common_trap: Common facts do not create a common undivided interest.

rules.title: Supplemental Jurisdiction
rules.prompt_question: When does supplemental jurisdiction exist?
rules.rule_statement: Supplemental jurisdiction exists when the additional claim and the original claim arise from a common nucleus of operative fact such that they form part of the same case or controversy.
rules.application_example: A related state law negligence claim may ride with a federal civil rights claim arising from the same event.
rules.common_trap: Claims must be factually connected, not merely loosely related.

rules.title: Supplemental Jurisdiction Limitation in Diversity Cases
rules.prompt_question: What is a major limitation on supplemental jurisdiction in diversity cases?
rules.rule_statement: In diversity cases, supplemental jurisdiction generally cannot be used to override complete diversity for certain claims by plaintiffs against persons made parties under the federal joinder rules.
rules.application_example: A plaintiff usually cannot use supplemental jurisdiction to sue a nondiverse impleaded party if the statute bars it.
rules.common_trap: This limitation focuses on certain claims by plaintiffs, not all claims by all parties.

rules.title: Declining Supplemental Jurisdiction
rules.prompt_question: When may a federal court decline to exercise supplemental jurisdiction?
rules.rule_statement: A federal court may decline supplemental jurisdiction if the state law claim is novel or complex, substantially predominates, or all claims supporting original jurisdiction have been dismissed.
rules.application_example: If all federal claims are dismissed early, the court may dismiss the remaining state claims.
rules.common_trap: The court may have power over the claim and still decline to hear it.

rules.title: Removal
rules.prompt_question: When may a defendant remove a case from state court to federal court?
rules.rule_statement: A defendant may remove a civil action from state court to federal court if the federal court would have had original subject matter jurisdiction over the action.
rules.application_example: A defendant may remove a federal question case filed in state court.
rules.common_trap: A plaintiff cannot remove.

rules.title: Unanimity Rule for Removal
rules.prompt_question: What is the unanimity rule for removal?
rules.rule_statement: All properly joined and served defendants generally must join in or consent to removal.
keywords: join in or consent to removal
rules.application_example: If one served defendant refuses to consent, removal is usually defective.
rules.common_trap: Only properly joined and served defendants count for the unanimity rule.

rules.title: Forum Defendant Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the home state defendant limitation on diversity removal?
rules.rule_statement: A diversity case generally may not be removed if any properly joined and served defendant is a citizen of the state in which the action was filed.
rules.application_example: A Texas defendant sued in Texas state court generally cannot remove on diversity grounds.
rules.common_trap: This rule does not apply to federal question removal.

rules.title: Time for Removal
rules.prompt_question: When must a notice of removal generally be filed?
rules.rule_statement: A notice of removal generally must be filed within 30 days after the defendant receives the initial pleading or is served, depending on the procedural posture.
rules.application_example: A defendant who waits beyond the 30 day period usually loses the right to remove.
rules.common_trap: Watch the trigger date carefully on exams.

rules.title: One Year Limit in Diversity Cases
rules.prompt_question: What is the one year limitation on diversity removal?
rules.rule_statement: A diversity case generally may not be removed more than one year after commencement of the action unless an exception applies.
rules.application_example: If the case only becomes removable on diversity grounds after one year, removal is usually barred.
rules.common_trap: This is a diversity specific limit.

rules.title: Remand for Lack of Subject Matter Jurisdiction
rules.prompt_question: When must a removed case be remanded?
rules.rule_statement: A removed case must be remanded if the federal court lacks subject matter jurisdiction.
rules.application_example: If complete diversity never existed, the federal court must remand.
rules.common_trap: Subject matter jurisdiction defects are not waived.

rules.title: Remand for Procedural Defect
rules.prompt_question: How are procedural defects in removal handled?
rules.rule_statement: Procedural defects in removal must be raised by timely motion to remand or they are generally waived.
rules.application_example: If the unanimity rule was violated, the plaintiff must timely object or the defect may be waived.
rules.common_trap: Do not confuse waivable removal defects with nonwaivable jurisdiction defects.

rules.title: Traditional Bases of Personal Jurisdiction
rules.prompt_question: What traditional bases support personal jurisdiction?
rules.rule_statement: Traditional bases of personal jurisdiction include service on the defendant while present in the forum, domicile in the forum, consent, and in some cases waiver.
rules.application_example: A defendant served while voluntarily present in the forum is usually subject to personal jurisdiction there.
rules.common_trap: Traditional bases can support jurisdiction even without a separate minimum contacts analysis.

rules.title: Minimum Contacts
rules.prompt_question: What is required for personal jurisdiction under minimum contacts?
rules.rule_statement: Personal jurisdiction is proper when the defendant has minimum contacts with the forum such that exercising jurisdiction does not offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice.
rules.application_example: A defendant who purposefully directs commercial activity at the forum may have minimum contacts there.
rules.common_trap: The plaintiff's unilateral contact with the forum does not count as the defendant's contact.

rules.title: Purposeful Availment
rules.prompt_question: What is purposeful availment in personal jurisdiction analysis?
rules.rule_statement: Purposeful availment means the defendant deliberately engaged with the forum, invoking the benefits and protections of its laws, rather than being haled there by random, fortuitous, or unilateral acts.
rules.application_example: A business that targets repeated sales to forum residents may have purposefully availed itself of the forum.
rules.common_trap: Mere foreseeability of a product traveling to the forum is not always enough.

rules.title: Specific Jurisdiction
rules.prompt_question: When does specific jurisdiction exist?
rules.rule_statement: Specific jurisdiction exists when the defendant purposefully avails itself of the forum and the claim arises out of or relates to the defendant's forum contacts.
rules.application_example: A suit over a defective product sold into the forum may support specific jurisdiction if the sale is tied to the forum contacts.
rules.common_trap: Unrelated contacts do not support specific jurisdiction.

rules.title: General Jurisdiction
rules.prompt_question: When does general jurisdiction exist?
rules.rule_statement: General jurisdiction exists when the defendant is essentially at home in the forum, so the court may hear any claim against that defendant.
rules.application_example: An individual is generally at home where domiciled, and a corporation is generally at home where incorporated and where it has its principal place of business.
rules.common_trap: Substantial business alone does not automatically make a corporation at home.

rules.title: Fairness Factors
rules.prompt_question: What fairness factors may a court consider in personal jurisdiction analysis?
rules.rule_statement: A court may consider the burden on the defendant, the forum state's interest, the plaintiff's interest in convenient relief, the interstate judicial system's interest in efficiency, and shared policy interests of the states.
rules.application_example: Even where contacts exist, extreme unfairness may weigh against exercising jurisdiction.
rules.common_trap: Fairness factors usually do not replace the need for minimum contacts.

rules.title: Long Arm Statute
rules.prompt_question: Why is a long arm statute relevant to personal jurisdiction?
rules.rule_statement: A court ordinarily needs a statute authorizing personal jurisdiction, such as a long arm statute, in addition to compliance with constitutional due process.
rules.application_example: Even if due process would allow jurisdiction, the forum must also have statutory authorization.
rules.common_trap: Always ask both questions: statutory reach and constitutional validity.

rules.title: Due Process Notice
rules.prompt_question: What notice is required by due process?
rules.rule_statement: Due process requires notice reasonably calculated under the circumstances to apprise interested parties of the action and give them an opportunity to be heard.
rules.application_example: Mailing notice to the party's last known address may satisfy due process if reasonable under the circumstances.
rules.common_trap: Due process focuses on reasonable method, not guaranteed actual notice in every case.

rules.title: Service of Process
rules.prompt_question: What is required for proper service of process?
rules.rule_statement: Proper service of process requires delivery of the summons and complaint by a method authorized by rule and reasonably calculated to notify the defendant.
rules.application_example: Personal service of the summons and complaint is ordinarily valid service.
rules.common_trap: Actual notice does not always cure defective service.

rules.title: Waiver of Service
rules.prompt_question: What is the effect of waiving service?
rules.rule_statement: A defendant who waives formal service does not waive objections to venue or jurisdiction and receives additional time to respond.
rules.application_example: A defendant may agree to waive formal service but still contest personal jurisdiction later.
rules.common_trap: Waiving service is not the same as consenting to jurisdiction.

rules.title: Venue
rules.prompt_question: When is venue proper in federal court?
rules.rule_statement: Venue is proper in a district where any defendant resides if all defendants reside in the same state, or in a district where a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred.
rules.application_example: Venue may be proper where the contract was negotiated and breached if a substantial part of the events occurred there.
rules.common_trap: Venue is separate from personal jurisdiction and subject matter jurisdiction.

rules.title: Corporate Residence for Venue
rules.prompt_question: How does a corporation reside for venue purposes?
rules.rule_statement: A corporate defendant is deemed to reside in any district where it is subject to personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question.
rules.application_example: If a corporation is subject to personal jurisdiction in a district for the claim, it generally resides there for venue purposes.
rules.common_trap: Venue residence is not always the same as diversity citizenship.

rules.title: Transfer for Convenience
rules.prompt_question: When may a federal court transfer a case for convenience?
rules.rule_statement: A federal court may transfer a case to another district where the action might have been brought if transfer serves the convenience of parties and witnesses and the interests of justice.
rules.application_example: A case may be transferred to the district where most witnesses and documents are located.
rules.common_trap: The transferee court must be one where the case could originally have been filed.

rules.title: Transfer or Dismiss When Venue Is Wrong
rules.prompt_question: What may a federal court do when venue is improper?
rules.rule_statement: When venue is improper, the court may dismiss or transfer the case to a proper district if justice so requires.
rules.application_example: If the case was filed in the wrong district, the court may transfer rather than dismiss to avoid wasting time.
rules.common_trap: Do not confuse transfer for convenience with transfer to cure improper venue.

rules.title: Forum Non Conveniens
rules.prompt_question: When may a court dismiss under forum non conveniens?
rules.rule_statement: A court may dismiss under forum non conveniens when another available and adequate forum is substantially more convenient and transfer is not available.
rules.application_example: A federal court may dismiss in favor of a foreign country's courts if that forum is adequate and clearly more convenient.
rules.common_trap: Forum non conveniens usually leads to dismissal, not transfer.

rules.title: Erie Doctrine
rules.prompt_question: What law does a federal court apply in a diversity case?
rules.rule_statement: In a diversity case, a federal court applies federal procedural law and state substantive law.
rules.application_example: A federal court in diversity uses the Federal Rules for procedure but state negligence law for substance.
rules.common_trap: Not every issue labeled procedural by a party is automatically treated as federal procedure.

rules.title: Examples of State Substantive Law
rules.prompt_question: What kinds of issues are usually treated as state substantive law in diversity cases?
rules.rule_statement: State substantive law generally includes elements of claims and defenses, statutes of limitation, choice of law rules, and rules affecting primary conduct or outcome in a significant way.
rules.application_example: A federal court sitting in diversity generally applies the forum state's choice of law rules.
rules.common_trap: Students often forget that statutes of limitation are usually treated as substantive for Erie purposes.

rules.title: General Pleading Standard
rules.prompt_question: What must a complaint contain to satisfy the federal pleading standard?
rules.rule_statement: A complaint must contain a short and plain statement showing that the pleader is entitled to relief and enough factual matter to state a plausible claim for relief.
rules.application_example: A complaint stating only that the defendant acted unlawfully, without factual support, may be dismissed.
rules.common_trap: Legal conclusions alone are not enough.

rules.title: Fraud Pleading Standard
rules.prompt_question: How must fraud be pleaded in federal court?
rules.rule_statement: Fraud must be pleaded with particularity, although conditions of mind such as malice, intent, knowledge, and other mental states may be alleged generally.
rules.application_example: The pleading should identify the misrepresentation, speaker, timing, and content with specificity.
rules.common_trap: Fraud is not governed by the ordinary notice pleading standard alone.

rules.title: Special Damages
rules.prompt_question: How must special damages be pleaded?
rules.rule_statement: Special damages must be specifically stated.
rules.application_example: A claim for lost profits usually must be pleaded with sufficient specificity.
rules.common_trap: Do not lump special damages into vague general pleading.

rules.title: Rule 11
rules.prompt_question: What certifications does a lawyer make by signing a federal pleading, motion, or paper?
rules.rule_statement: By signing a federal paper, a lawyer certifies that it is not presented for an improper purpose, that legal contentions are warranted by law or a nonfrivolous argument, and that factual contentions have or likely will have evidentiary support.
rules.application_example: A filing submitted only to harass the opponent may violate Rule 11.
rules.common_trap: Rule 11 does not require that the signer ultimately win the case.

rules.title: Amendment as a Matter of Course
rules.prompt_question: When may a party amend a pleading once as a matter of course?
rules.rule_statement: A party may amend a pleading once as a matter of course within the time allowed by the federal rules before needing leave of court or consent of the opposing party.
rules.application_example: A plaintiff may amend early in the case without asking the judge for permission.
rules.common_trap: After the as-of-right window closes, leave or consent is usually required.

rules.title: Leave to Amend
rules.prompt_question: When should leave to amend generally be granted?
rules.rule_statement: Leave to amend should be freely given when justice so requires.
rules.application_example: Courts often permit amendment absent undue delay, bad faith, repeated failure, prejudice, or futility.
rules.common_trap: Freely given does not mean automatic.

rules.title: Relation Back
rules.prompt_question: When does an amended pleading relate back to the date of the original pleading?
rules.rule_statement: An amended pleading relates back when it arises out of the same conduct, transaction, or occurrence set out in the original pleading.
rules.application_example: An amendment adding a new negligence theory based on the same accident may relate back.
rules.common_trap: A claim based on a different event does not relate back.

rules.title: Relation Back When Changing Parties
rules.prompt_question: When may an amendment changing a party relate back?
rules.rule_statement: An amendment changing a party may relate back when it arises from the same conduct, transaction, or occurrence and the new party received timely notice and knew or should have known that but for a mistake it would have been named originally.
rules.application_example: If the plaintiff sued the wrong related corporate entity by mistake, relation back may apply if notice and mistake requirements are satisfied.
rules.common_trap: Lack of mistake can defeat relation back.

rules.title: Required Joinder
rules.prompt_question: When must a party be joined if feasible?
rules.rule_statement: A party must be joined if feasible when complete relief cannot be accorded without that party or the absent party claims an interest that may be impaired or may expose existing parties to multiple or inconsistent obligations.
rules.application_example: A co-owner of disputed property may be a required party if the action would affect that ownership interest.
rules.common_trap: First ask whether the party is required, then whether joinder is feasible.

rules.title: When a Required Party Cannot Be Joined
rules.prompt_question: What should a court do if a required party cannot be joined?
rules.rule_statement: If a required party cannot be joined, the court decides whether to proceed without that party or dismiss the action after considering fairness and practical factors.
rules.application_example: If joinder would destroy diversity, the court decides whether the case can proceed in equity and good conscience without the party.
rules.common_trap: A required party is not automatically an indispensable party.

rules.title: Permissive Joinder
rules.prompt_question: When may parties be permissively joined in one action?
rules.rule_statement: Parties may be permissively joined if their claims arise out of the same transaction or occurrence and share at least one common question of law or fact.
keywords: common question of law or fact
rules.application_example: Passengers injured in the same collision may join in one action if common issues exist.
rules.common_trap: A common legal issue alone is not enough without the transaction or occurrence link.

rules.title: Compulsory Counterclaim
rules.prompt_question: When is a counterclaim compulsory?
rules.rule_statement: A counterclaim is compulsory if it arises out of the same transaction or occurrence as the opposing party's claim.
rules.application_example: A defendant's injury claim from the same car accident usually must be brought as a compulsory counterclaim.
rules.common_trap: A compulsory counterclaim not raised is generally lost.

rules.title: Permissive Counterclaim
rules.prompt_question: When is a counterclaim permissive?
rules.rule_statement: A counterclaim is permissive if it does not arise out of the same transaction or occurrence as the opposing party's claim.
keywords: does not arise out of same transaction or occurrence
rules.application_example: A debt claim unrelated to the plaintiff's tort claim may be a permissive counterclaim.
rules.common_trap: Permissive counterclaims do not have to be asserted in the same action.

rules.title: Crossclaim
rules.prompt_question: When may a party assert a crossclaim?
rules.rule_statement: A crossclaim may be asserted against a coparty if it arises out of the same transaction or occurrence as the original action or of a counterclaim in the action.
rules.application_example: One defendant may assert indemnity against a codefendant arising out of the same accident.
rules.common_trap: Crossclaims are generally permissive, not compulsory.

rules.title: Impleader
rules.prompt_question: When may a defending party implead a third party?
rules.rule_statement: A defending party may implead a nonparty who may be liable to the defending party for all or part of the plaintiff's claim against that defending party.
keywords: all or part of plaintiff claim
rules.application_example: A defendant may implead an indemnitor or joint tortfeasor for contribution if allowed.
rules.common_trap: Impleader requires derivative liability to the defendant, not just direct liability to the plaintiff.

rules.title: Intervention of Right
rules.prompt_question: When is intervention of right available?
rules.rule_statement: Intervention of right is available when the applicant claims an interest relating to the property or transaction, disposition may impair that interest, and existing parties do not adequately represent the interest.
rules.application_example: A property claimant may intervene where the suit threatens that interest and current parties will not protect it.
rules.common_trap: The interest must be sufficient and practical impairment matters.

rules.title: Permissive Intervention
rules.prompt_question: When may permissive intervention be allowed?
rules.rule_statement: Permissive intervention may be allowed when the applicant's claim or defense and the main action share a common question of law or fact.
keywords: common question of law or fact
rules.application_example: A party with a closely related legal issue may be allowed to intervene if doing so will not unduly delay or prejudice the original parties.
rules.common_trap: Unlike intervention of right, this is discretionary.

rules.title: Interpleader
rules.prompt_question: What is interpleader?
rules.rule_statement: Interpleader allows a stakeholder holding property claimed by multiple claimants to join them in one action to avoid multiple liability or inconsistent obligations.
rules.application_example: An insurer faced with competing claims to policy proceeds may file interpleader.
rules.common_trap: The point is to protect the stakeholder from inconsistent exposure.

rules.title: Class Action Prerequisites
rules.prompt_question: What are the basic prerequisites for a class action?
rules.rule_statement: A class action requires numerosity, commonality, typicality, and adequacy of representation.
rules.application_example: A large group of consumers allegedly harmed by the same practice may satisfy these prerequisites.
rules.common_trap: Meeting these four alone is not enough. The case must also fit a class action type.

rules.title: Class Action Types
rules.prompt_question: What additional showing is needed after the four class prerequisites are met?
rules.rule_statement: After satisfying numerosity, commonality, typicality, and adequacy, the proposed class must also fit within an authorized class action category under the federal rules.
rules.application_example: A damages class often requires predominance and superiority.
rules.common_trap: Do not stop your analysis after the first four requirements.

rules.title: Scope of Discovery
rules.prompt_question: What information is generally discoverable in federal civil litigation?
rules.rule_statement: Parties may obtain discovery regarding any nonprivileged matter relevant to a party's claim or defense and proportional to the needs of the case.
rules.application_example: Relevant emails may be discoverable if the request is proportionate and not privileged.
rules.common_trap: Discoverability is broader than admissibility.

rules.title: Work Product Doctrine
rules.prompt_question: What is the work product doctrine?
rules.rule_statement: Work product protects materials prepared in anticipation of litigation from discovery unless the requesting party shows substantial need and undue hardship in obtaining the substantial equivalent by other means.
rules.application_example: An attorney's interview notes may be protected work product.
rules.common_trap: Opinion work product receives especially strong protection.

rules.title: Privilege
rules.prompt_question: How does privilege affect discovery?
rules.rule_statement: Privileged matter is not discoverable even if it is relevant and proportional.
rules.application_example: Attorney-client privileged communications are generally protected from discovery.
rules.common_trap: Students often mix privilege with work product. They are different protections.

rules.title: Mandatory Initial Disclosures
rules.prompt_question: What are mandatory initial disclosures?
rules.rule_statement: Parties must provide certain basic information early in the case without awaiting a discovery request, including likely witnesses, relevant documents, damages information, and insurance information.
rules.application_example: A party must identify people likely to have discoverable information early in the case.
rules.common_trap: These disclosures are automatic in covered cases.

rules.title: Expert Disclosures
rules.prompt_question: What must parties generally disclose about expert testimony?
rules.rule_statement: Parties generally must disclose expert witnesses and provide the required expert information or reports within the time set by rule or court order.
rules.application_example: A retained expert usually requires a formal written report.
rules.common_trap: Expert disclosure obligations are more demanding than ordinary witness disclosures.

rules.title: Deposition Limits
rules.prompt_question: How are depositions generally limited in federal practice?
rules.rule_statement: Depositions are subject to numerical, temporal, and proportionality limits under the federal rules and court supervision.
rules.application_example: A party seeking unusually broad deposition practice may need court approval.
rules.common_trap: Do not assume unlimited depositions are automatically allowed.

rules.title: Discovery Sanctions
rules.prompt_question: When may a court impose discovery sanctions?
rules.rule_statement: A court may impose sanctions for failure to comply with discovery obligations or court orders, including orders compelling discovery.
rules.application_example: If a party disobeys an order to produce records, the court may impose sanctions.
rules.common_trap: Sanctions can be severe when noncompliance is willful or repeated.

rules.title: Pretrial Orders
rules.prompt_question: What is the effect of a pretrial order?
rules.rule_statement: A pretrial order controls the course of the action unless modified by the court.
keywords: controls the course of the action
rules.application_example: A claim omitted from the final pretrial order may be difficult to pursue at trial.
rules.common_trap: Students underestimate how powerful the pretrial order is.

rules.title: Seventh Amendment Jury Trial Right
rules.prompt_question: When does the Seventh Amendment right to a jury trial apply in civil cases?
rules.rule_statement: The Seventh Amendment preserves the right to a jury trial in federal civil actions for legal claims, but not for purely equitable claims.
rules.application_example: A damages action usually carries a jury right, but a pure injunction action usually does not.
rules.common_trap: The remedy sought matters a lot.

rules.title: Jury Demand and Waiver
rules.prompt_question: How is the right to jury trial preserved or waived?
rules.rule_statement: A party generally must timely demand a jury trial or the right is waived.
rules.application_example: If no timely jury demand is made, the case may proceed as a bench trial.
rules.common_trap: Having a jury right is not enough. The right must be preserved.

rules.title: Motion to Dismiss for Lack of Subject Matter Jurisdiction
rules.prompt_question: What does a Rule 12(b)(1) motion challenge?
rules.rule_statement: A Rule 12(b)(1) motion challenges the court's subject matter jurisdiction.
rules.application_example: A defendant may move to dismiss if diversity is lacking.
rules.common_trap: Subject matter jurisdiction defects are never waived.

rules.title: Motion to Dismiss for Lack of Personal Jurisdiction
rules.prompt_question: What does a Rule 12(b)(2) motion challenge?
rules.rule_statement: A Rule 12(b)(2) motion challenges the court's personal jurisdiction over the defendant.
rules.application_example: A nonresident defendant may argue insufficient forum contacts.
rules.common_trap: Personal jurisdiction can be waived if not timely raised.

rules.title: Motion to Dismiss for Improper Venue
rules.prompt_question: What does a Rule 12(b)(3) motion challenge?
rules.rule_statement: A Rule 12(b)(3) motion challenges venue as improper.
rules.application_example: A defendant may move if the suit was filed in the wrong district.
rules.common_trap: Venue objections can be waived if not timely asserted.

rules.title: Insufficient Process and Insufficient Service of Process
rules.prompt_question: What do Rule 12(b)(4) and Rule 12(b)(5) challenge?
rules.rule_statement: Rule 12(b)(4) challenges defects in process itself, while Rule 12(b)(5) challenges the manner or sufficiency of service of process.
rules.application_example: A missing summons may raise process issues, while defective delivery may raise service issues.
rules.common_trap: Students often collapse these two defenses into one.

rules.title: Failure to State a Claim
rules.prompt_question: When should a Rule 12(b)(6) motion be granted?
rules.rule_statement: A Rule 12(b)(6) motion should be granted when the complaint fails to allege sufficient facts to state a plausible claim for relief.
rules.application_example: A complaint containing only labels and conclusions may be dismissed.
rules.common_trap: The court generally assumes well pleaded factual allegations are true at this stage.

rules.title: Failure to Join a Required Party
rules.prompt_question: What does a Rule 12(b)(7) motion challenge?
rules.rule_statement: A Rule 12(b)(7) motion challenges the failure to join a party required under the joinder rules.
rules.application_example: A defendant may argue the action cannot fairly proceed without an absent required party.
rules.common_trap: This defense links directly to the required joinder analysis.

rules.title: Waiver of Certain Rule 12 Defenses
rules.prompt_question: Which Rule 12 defenses are generally waived if not timely raised?
rules.rule_statement: Personal jurisdiction, venue, process, and service of process defenses are generally waived if not timely asserted.
rules.application_example: A defendant who answers without raising improper venue usually waives that defense.
rules.common_trap: Subject matter jurisdiction is different because it is not waived.

rules.title: Summary Judgment
rules.prompt_question: When is summary judgment proper?
rules.rule_statement: Summary judgment is proper when there is no genuine dispute of material fact and the moving party is entitled to judgment as a matter of law.
keywords: judgment as a matter of law
rules.application_example: If no reasonable factfinder could find for the nonmoving party on the evidence presented, summary judgment may be granted.
rules.common_trap: A factual dispute matters only if it is both genuine and material.

rules.title: Default
rules.prompt_question: When may default be entered?
rules.rule_statement: Default may be entered when a party fails to plead or otherwise defend.
rules.application_example: A defendant who never answers after proper service may be defaulted.
rules.common_trap: Default is distinct from default judgment.

rules.title: Default Judgment
rules.prompt_question: What is default judgment?
rules.rule_statement: Default judgment is the judgment entered against a party after default, subject to the federal rules and any required procedures.
rules.application_example: After entry of default, the plaintiff may seek judgment if procedural requirements are satisfied.
rules.common_trap: Default does not automatically equal final judgment without the next step.

rules.title: Judgment as a Matter of Law
rules.prompt_question: When may a court grant judgment as a matter of law?
rules.rule_statement: A court may grant judgment as a matter of law when a reasonable jury would not have a legally sufficient evidentiary basis to find for the nonmoving party.
keywords: judgment as a matter of law
rules.application_example: If the plaintiff presents no evidence on an essential element, JMOL may be proper.
rules.common_trap: JMOL is tied to jury trials, not bench trials.

rules.title: Renewed Judgment as a Matter of Law
rules.prompt_question: What is required for a renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law?
rules.rule_statement: A renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law may be made after verdict only if the party made a JMOL motion before the case was submitted to the jury.
rules.application_example: If the defendant never moved for JMOL during trial, renewed JMOL is unavailable after verdict.
rules.common_trap: The earlier motion is the preservation step.

rules.title: Motion for New Trial
rules.prompt_question: When may a court grant a new trial?
rules.rule_statement: A court may grant a new trial for reasons such as prejudicial error, misconduct, newly discovered evidence, or a verdict against the great weight of the evidence.
rules.application_example: A trial infected by serious evidentiary error may justify a new trial.
rules.common_trap: A new trial motion gives the judge broader discretion than JMOL.

rules.title: Relief from Judgment
rules.prompt_question: When may a court relieve a party from a final judgment or order?
rules.rule_statement: A court may relieve a party from a final judgment or order for reasons such as mistake, excusable neglect, newly discovered evidence, fraud, or other grounds recognized by rule.
rules.application_example: A party may seek relief if judgment was entered because of excusable neglect or fraud.
rules.common_trap: This relief is exceptional, not routine.

rules.title: Entry of Judgment
rules.prompt_question: What is the significance of entry of judgment?
rules.rule_statement: Entry of judgment makes the court's decision operative and starts important post judgment and appellate deadlines.
rules.application_example: The time to appeal generally runs from entry of judgment.
rules.common_trap: Watch the difference between verdict and judgment.

rules.title: Preliminary Injunction
rules.prompt_question: What is a preliminary injunction?
rules.rule_statement: A preliminary injunction is provisional equitable relief entered before final judgment to preserve the status quo or prevent irreparable harm while the case is pending.
rules.application_example: A court may issue a preliminary injunction to stop ongoing disclosure of trade secrets during litigation.
rules.common_trap: This is not a final judgment on the merits.

rules.title: Temporary Restraining Order
rules.prompt_question: What is a temporary restraining order?
rules.rule_statement: A temporary restraining order is short term provisional relief designed to preserve the status quo until the court can consider a request for preliminary injunction.
rules.application_example: A TRO may be sought to prevent immediate destruction of disputed property before a hearing can be held.
rules.common_trap: A TRO is narrower and more temporary than a preliminary injunction.

rules.title: Final Judgment Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the final judgment rule?
rules.rule_statement: As a general rule, only final judgments that resolve all claims as to all parties are immediately appealable.
rules.application_example: If one claim remains unresolved, there is usually no immediate appeal as of right.
rules.common_trap: Many important interlocutory rulings are still not immediately appealable.

rules.title: Interlocutory Appeals
rules.prompt_question: When may an interlocutory order be appealed before final judgment?
rules.rule_statement: Certain interlocutory orders may be appealed before final judgment, including orders regarding injunctions and other orders made immediately appealable by statute or recognized exception.
rules.application_example: An order granting or denying a preliminary injunction is usually immediately appealable.
rules.common_trap: Not every pretrial order qualifies.

rules.title: Collateral Order Doctrine
rules.prompt_question: What is the collateral order doctrine?
rules.rule_statement: The collateral order doctrine permits immediate appeal of a narrow class of orders that conclusively resolve an important issue separate from the merits and would be effectively unreviewable after final judgment.
rules.application_example: An order denying a certain immunity claim may sometimes qualify under the doctrine.
rules.common_trap: This doctrine is narrow and should not be overapplied.

rules.title: Review of Legal Conclusions
rules.prompt_question: How are pure legal conclusions reviewed on appeal?
rules.rule_statement: Pure legal conclusions are generally reviewed de novo.
rules.application_example: An appellate court independently reviews the trial court's interpretation of a statute.
rules.common_trap: Do not apply de novo review to every kind of ruling.

rules.title: Review of Findings of Fact
rules.prompt_question: How are findings of fact generally reviewed on appeal?
rules.rule_statement: Findings of fact are generally reviewed with deference and are not reversed unless the applicable standard for disturbing factual findings is met.
rules.application_example: An appellate court usually defers to trial level fact finding unless the relevant standard allows reversal.
rules.common_trap: Do not treat factual review the same as legal review.

rules.title: Abuse of Discretion Review
rules.prompt_question: What kinds of trial court rulings are often reviewed for abuse of discretion?
rules.rule_statement: Many case management, evidentiary, discovery, and similar discretionary rulings are reviewed for abuse of discretion.
rules.application_example: A trial court's management of discovery disputes is often reviewed under this standard.
rules.common_trap: Abuse of discretion is a deferential standard.

rules.title: Claim Preclusion
rules.prompt_question: What are the requirements for claim preclusion?
rules.rule_statement: Claim preclusion bars a later claim when there was a valid final judgment on the merits, the cases involve the same parties or their privies, and the later claim arises from the same transaction or occurrence.
keywords: valid final judgment on the merits
rules.application_example: A plaintiff who litigated a contract dispute to final judgment cannot later sue the same defendant on another theory arising from the same transaction.
rules.common_trap: Claim preclusion bars claims that were or could have been brought in the first action.

rules.title: Issue Preclusion
rules.prompt_question: What are the requirements for issue preclusion?
rules.rule_statement: Issue preclusion bars relitigation of an issue when the same issue was actually litigated, actually decided, essential to a valid final judgment, and asserted against a party who had a full and fair opportunity to litigate.
rules.application_example: If negligence was fully litigated and decided in the first case, that issue generally cannot be relitigated later against the same party.
rules.common_trap: Issue preclusion applies to issues, not entire claims.

rules.title: Judgment on the Merits
rules.prompt_question: What kind of judgment satisfies the on-the-merits requirement for claim preclusion?
rules.rule_statement: Claim preclusion generally requires a valid final judgment on the merits, meaning a judgment that finally resolves the claim in a manner treated as merits based under preclusion law.
rules.application_example: A dismissal with prejudice is commonly treated as on the merits for preclusion purposes.
rules.common_trap: Not every dismissal qualifies as a merits judgment.

rules.title: Nonmutual Issue Preclusion Caution
rules.prompt_question: What should you remember about nonmutual issue preclusion?
rules.rule_statement: Nonmutual issue preclusion may be allowed in some circumstances, but fairness limitations apply and exam analysis must consider whether the party against whom preclusion is asserted had a full and fair opportunity to litigate.
rules.application_example: A new plaintiff may sometimes seek to preclude a defendant from relitigating an issue already lost against another party, subject to fairness concerns.
rules.common_trap: Do not apply nonmutual preclusion mechanically without fairness analysis.
